    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "I'm starting my first fantasy novel - 'The Last Ember'.\n\nMain plot: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n\nNeed to:\n\n- Develop main character backstory (orphan, raised by master blacksmith)\n- Create magic system based on metal and fire\n- Map out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n- Write character profiles for the antagonist (The Shadow King)\n- Outline first three chapters\n- Research medieval blacksmithing techniques\n- Design the prophecy that drives the plot"
    },
    {
      "role": "assistant",
      "content": "I've created your new BuildOS project **The Last Ember** (ID: 04af5167-e41f-4d10-931b-c257c12767bb).\n\nHere's what was set up based on your description:\n\n- **Project details**: Type `project.creative.novel`, description matching the main plot, tagged as personal/medium-scale/planning stage, starting ~now.\n\n- **1 Goal**: \"Complete first draft of 'The Last Ember'\" — all tasks nested under it.\n\n- **7 Tasks**:\n\n- Map out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n\n- Outline first three chapters\n\n- Research medieval blacksmithing techniques\n\n- Design the prophecy that drives the plot\n\n- **1 Context Document**: \"The Last Ember - Project Overview\" with your plot summary and needs list.\n\nYou're now focused on this project.\n\nWhat next?\n\nStart research on blacksmithing, outline chapters, or something else?"
    },
    {
      "role": "user",
      "content": "Finished chapter 2 today - 4,500 words. The scene where Elena discovers the dragon forge went really well.\n\nIssues to address:\n\n- Need to strengthen the dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\n- The pacing in the middle feels slow\n- Add more sensory details about the forge\n\nChapter 3 plans:\n\n- Elena's first attempt at magical forging\n- Introduce the Shadow King's herald\n- Foreshadow the prophecy\n\nAlso need to go back and fix continuity issue - Elena's age mentioned as 16 in chapter 1 but 17 in chapter 2."
    }
  ],
  "tool_definitions": [
    {
      "type": "function",
      "function": {
        "name": "skill_load",
        "parameters": {
          "type": "object",
          "required": [
            "skill"
          ],
          "properties": {
            "skill": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Canonical skill id such as \"project_creation\", \"calendar_management\", \"task_management\", or \"document_workspace\". Legacy dotted skill aliases also work during migration."
            },
            "format": {
              "enum": [
                "short",
                "full"
              ],
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Short returns a compact summary; full returns the full playbook."
            },
            "include_examples": {
              "type": "boolean",
              "description": "Include examples when available."
            }
          }
        },
        "description": "Load one BuildOS skill playbook by skill id. Use this when the task is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong and you need workflow guidance before choosing tools."
      }
    },
    {
      "type": "function",
      "function": {
        "name": "tool_search",
        "parameters": {
          "type": "object",
          "properties": {
            "kind": {
              "enum": [
                "read",
                "write"
              ],
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Optional read/write filter. Prefer this when you know whether you need a mutation or a lookup."
            },
            "group": {
              "enum": [
                "onto",
                "util",
                "cal"
              ],
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Optional top-level tool family filter. Use this to narrow the search space once you know the tool family."
            },
            "limit": {
              "type": "integer",
              "description": "Maximum number of matches to return."
            },
            "query": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Natural-language description of the operation you need, not project/task content. Good examples: \"workspace overview\", \"update existing task state\", or \"move document in tree\". Bad example: \"chapter 3 plans\" when you are trying to find project data."
            },
            "entity": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Optional entity filter such as \"task\", \"project\", or \"document\". For example: entity=\"task\" with kind=\"write\" for task mutations."
            },
            "capability": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Optional BuildOS capability id or path such as \"overview\", \"project_creation\", or \"capabilities.calendar\". Prefer this when the capability family is already clear."
            }
          }
        },
        "description": "Discover candidate BuildOS tools on demand. Use this only when the exact op is still unknown after context and skill guidance. Search for the operation you need, not workspace data."
      }
    },
    {
      "type": "function",
      "function": {
        "name": "tool_schema",
        "parameters": {
          "type": "object",
          "required": [
            "op"
          ],
          "properties": {
            "op": {
              "type": "string",
              "minLength": 1,
              "description": "Canonical operation name such as \"onto.task.update\", \"util.project.overview\", or \"cal.event.create\"."
            },
            "include_schema": {
              "type": "boolean",
              "description": "Include the full JSON schema for the op arguments."
            },
            "include_examples": {
              "type": "boolean",
              "description": "Include example calls when available."
            }
          }
        },
        "description": "Return the exact schema, required arguments, examples, and usage guidance for one canonical BuildOS op."
      }
    },
    {
      "type": "function",
      "function": {
        "name": "change_chat_context",
        "parameters": {
          "type": "object",
          "required": [
            "target"
          ],
          "properties": {
            "reason": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Brief user-visible reason for the context change, grounded in the latest request."
            },
            "target": {
              "enum": [
                "global",
                "project"
              ],
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Target context. Use global for workspace/all-projects zoom-out. Use project for durable focus on one project."
            },
            "project_id": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Exact project UUID when zooming into a known project."
            },
            "project_query": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Project name or phrase to resolve when zooming into a project and project_id is unknown."
            }
          }
        },
        "description": "Change the durable chat context when the latest user request should strategically zoom into one project or zoom out to the workspace.\n\nUse this early in the turn before answering when:\n- The user explicitly asks to zoom, switch, focus, go to a project, or go back to all projects.\n- The current context is global/workspace and the latest request is primarily about one identifiable project, so project tools should be loaded for the rest of the turn.\n- The current context is one project and the latest request is clearly about a different project, and the user appears to be moving focus rather than doing a brief comparison.\n- The user asks about all projects, workspace status, cross-project priorities, or wants to zoom out.\n\nDo not use this for ambiguous project names, one-off comparisons across multiple projects, or brief mentions that can be answered from current context. For project zoom-in, pass project_id if known or project_query if the name needs resolution. Ambiguous or missing project matches return candidates and do not change context."
      }
    },
    {
      "name": "get_project_overview",
      "type": "function",
      "function": {
        "name": "get_project_overview",
        "parameters": {
          "type": "object",
          "properties": {
            "query": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Project name query when project_id is not yet known."
            },
            "project_id": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Exact project UUID when already known."
            }
          }
        },
        "description": "Get a BuildOS-native status summary for one project.\nUse this for questions like:\n- \"What's going on with 9takes?\"\n- \"Give me the current status of this project.\"\n- \"What is blocked or due soon in this project?\"\nPass project_id when known. If the user names a project but the ID is unknown, pass query and this op will try to resolve the project or return candidates."
      },
      "parameters": {
        "type": "object",
        "properties": {
          "query": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Project name query when project_id is not yet known."
          },
          "project_id": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Exact project UUID when already known."
          }
        }
      },
      "description": "Get a BuildOS-native status summary for one project.\nUse this for questions like:\n- \"What's going on with 9takes?\"\n- \"Give me the current status of this project.\"\n- \"What is blocked or due soon in this project?\"\nPass project_id when known. If the user names a project but the ID is unknown, pass query and this op will try to resolve the project or return candidates."
    },
    {
      "type": "function",
      "function": {
        "name": "get_onto_project_details",
        "parameters": {
          "type": "object",
          "required": [
            "project_id"
          ],
          "properties": {
            "project_id": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Project ID to retrieve"
            }
          }
        },
        "description": "Get complete details for a specific ontology project including properties and metadata.\nUse when you need full project information after identifying it with list_onto_projects."
      }
    },
    {
      "name": "search_project",
      "type": "function",
      "function": {
        "name": "search_project",
        "parameters": {
          "type": "object",
          "required": [
            "project_id",
            "query"
          ],
          "properties": {
            "limit": {
              "type": "number",
              "default": 10,
              "maximum": 25,
              "description": "Maximum number of results (capped at 25)"
            },
            "query": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Search text to match inside the specified project (required)"
            },
            "types": {
              "type": "array",
              "items": {
                "enum": [
                  "project",
                  "task",
                  "goal",
                  "plan",
                  "milestone",
                  "document",
                  "risk",
                  "requirement",
                  "image"
                ],
                "type": "string"
              },
              "description": "Optional entity type filters for narrowing results"
            },
            "project_id": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Project ID to scope the search to (required)"
            }
          }
        },
        "description": "Primary agent search inside one BuildOS project. Use this when a project_id is known or the chat is already scoped to a project.\nReturns typed ontology matches with snippets so you can quickly shortlist items before loading details."
      },
      "parameters": {
        "type": "object",
        "required": [
          "project_id",
          "query"
        ],
        "properties": {
          "limit": {
            "type": "number",
            "default": 10,
            "maximum": 25,
            "description": "Maximum number of results (capped at 25)"
          },
          "query": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Search text to match inside the specified project (required)"
          },
          "types": {
            "type": "array",
            "items": {
              "enum": [
                "project",
                "task",
                "goal",
                "plan",
                "milestone",
                "document",
                "risk",
                "requirement",
                "image"
              ],
              "type": "string"
            },
            "description": "Optional entity type filters for narrowing results"
          },
          "project_id": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Project ID to scope the search to (required)"
          }
        }
      },
      "description": "Primary agent search inside one BuildOS project. Use this when a project_id is known or the chat is already scoped to a project.\nReturns typed ontology matches with snippets so you can quickly shortlist items before loading details."
    },
    {
      "name": "list_onto_tasks",

        "name": "list_onto_tasks",

          "type": "object",

              "type": "number",
              "default": 20,
              "maximum": 50,
              "description": "Maximum tasks to return"
            },
            "state_key": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Filter by state (todo, in_progress, blocked, done)"
            },
            "project_id": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Filter tasks by project ID"
            }
          }
        },
        "description": "List tasks from the ontology system (onto_tasks table). Returns abbreviated task information including id, title, state, and type.\nUse this for queries about ontology tasks, plans with tasks, or project task lists."

        "properties": {
          "limit": {

            "description": "Filter by state (todo, in_progress, blocked, done)"
          },
          "project_id": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Filter tasks by project ID"
          }
        }
      },
      "description": "List tasks from the ontology system (onto_tasks table). Returns abbreviated task information including id, title, state, and type.\nUse this for queries about ontology tasks, plans with tasks, or project task lists."
    },

            "state_key": {

              "description": "Filter by document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"

              "description": "Filter documents by project ID"

        "description": "List documents from the ontology system (onto_documents table). Returns document summaries plus markdown heading outlines (H1/H2/H3), not full body content.\nUse get_onto_document_details when full document content is needed."

            "description": "Filter by document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"

          "project_id": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Filter documents by project ID"
          }
        }
      },
      "description": "List documents from the ontology system (onto_documents table). Returns document summaries plus markdown heading outlines (H1/H2/H3), not full body content.\nUse get_onto_document_details when full document content is needed."

    {
      "name": "create_onto_document",
      "type": "function",
      "function": {
        "name": "create_onto_document",
        "parameters": {
          "type": "object",
          "required": [
            "project_id",
            "title",
            "description"
          ],
          "properties": {
            "props": {
              "type": "object",
              "description": "Additional properties/metadata"
            },
            "title": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Document title (required)"
            },
            "parent": {
              "type": "object",
              "properties": {
                "id": {
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "kind": {
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "is_primary": {
                  "type": "boolean"
                }
              },
              "description": "Optional parent reference for semantic linking (legacy)"
            },
            "content": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Markdown body content stored in the content column"
            },
            "parents": {
              "type": "array",
              "items": {
                "type": "object",
                "properties": {
                  "id": {
                    "type": "string"
                  },
                  "kind": {
                    "type": "string"
                  },
                  "is_primary": {
                    "type": "boolean"
                  }
                }
              },
              "description": "Optional multiple semantic parents (legacy)"
            },
            "position": {
              "type": "number",
              "description": "Position among siblings (0-indexed). Omit to place at end."
            },
            "type_key": {
              "type": "string",
              "default": "document.default",
              "description": "Document type taxonomy (required): document.{family}[.{variant}]\nFamilies: context (project/brief), knowledge (research/brain_dump), notes (meeting_notes/rfc), spec (product/technical), reference (handbook/sop), intake (client/project).\nExamples: document.context.project, document.knowledge.research, document.spec.technical"
            },
            "parent_id": {
              "type": [
                "string",
                "null"
              ],
              "description": "Parent document ID to place this document under in the tree hierarchy. Null or omitted places at root level."
            },
            "state_key": {
              "type": "string",
              "default": "draft",
              "description": "Document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
            },
            "project_id": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Project UUID (required)"
            },
            "description": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Short summary of the document (required)"
            }
          }
        },
        "description": "Create a new document in the ontology system.\nUse for briefs, specs, context docs, or research artifacts linked to a project.\nDocuments are organized in a hierarchical tree structure. Use parent_id to place the document under a parent folder."
      },
      "parameters": {
        "type": "object",
        "required": [
          "project_id",
          "title",
          "description"
        ],
        "properties": {
          "props": {
            "type": "object",
            "description": "Additional properties/metadata"
          },
          "title": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Document title (required)"
          },
          "parent": {
            "type": "object",
            "properties": {
              "id": {
                "type": "string"
              },
              "kind": {
                "type": "string"
              },
              "is_primary": {
                "type": "boolean"
              }
            },
            "description": "Optional parent reference for semantic linking (legacy)"
          },
          "content": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Markdown body content stored in the content column"
          },
          "parents": {
            "type": "array",
            "items": {
              "type": "object",
              "properties": {
                "id": {
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "kind": {
                  "type": "string"
                },
                "is_primary": {
                  "type": "boolean"
                }
              }
            },
            "description": "Optional multiple semantic parents (legacy)"
          },
          "position": {
            "type": "number",
            "description": "Position among siblings (0-indexed). Omit to place at end."
          },
          "type_key": {
            "type": "string",
            "default": "document.default",
            "description": "Document type taxonomy (required): document.{family}[.{variant}]\nFamilies: context (project/brief), knowledge (research/brain_dump), notes (meeting_notes/rfc), spec (product/technical), reference (handbook/sop), intake (client/project).\nExamples: document.context.project, document.knowledge.research, document.spec.technical"
          },
          "parent_id": {
            "type": [
              "string",
              "null"
            ],
            "description": "Parent document ID to place this document under in the tree hierarchy. Null or omitted places at root level."
          },
          "state_key": {
            "type": "string",
            "default": "draft",
            "description": "Document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
          },
          "project_id": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Project UUID (required)"
          },
          "description": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Short summary of the document (required)"
          }
        }
      },
      "description": "Create a new document in the ontology system.\nUse for briefs, specs, context docs, or research artifacts linked to a project.\nDocuments are organized in a hierarchical tree structure. Use parent_id to place the document under a parent folder."
    },
    {
      "name": "update_onto_document",
      "type": "function",
      "function": {
        "name": "update_onto_document",
        "parameters": {
          "type": "object",
          "required": [
            "document_id"
          ],
          "properties": {
            "props": {
              "type": "object",
              "description": "Metadata fields to merge into document props"
            },
            "title": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "New document title"
            },
            "content": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Markdown content to store in the content column. Required when update_strategy is append or merge_llm."
            },
            "type_key": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "New document type key"
            },
            "state_key": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
            },
            "description": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Short summary of the document"
            },
            "document_id": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Document UUID (required)"
            },
            "update_strategy": {
              "enum": [
                "replace",
                "append",
                "merge_llm"
              ],
              "type": "string",
              "default": "replace",
              "description": "How to apply content: 'replace' (default), 'append', or 'merge_llm' to intelligently merge with existing content."
            },
            "merge_instructions": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Optional guidance when merging content (e.g., keep headers, preserve tables, integrate research notes). Used with append/merge_llm, but does not replace the required content."
            }
          },
          "additionalProperties": false
        },
        "description": "Update an existing ontology document. Use for edits to titles, states, body markdown, or metadata. For update_strategy \"append\" or \"merge_llm\", include non-empty content/body_markdown/markdown/body/text. merge_instructions alone is not content. Append example: update_onto_document({ document_id, content: \"## Progress Updates\\n\\n- Chapter 2 complete...\", update_strategy: \"append\", merge_instructions: \"Append under Progress Updates; preserve existing sections.\" })"
      },
      "parameters": {
        "type": "object",
        "required": [
          "document_id"
        ],
        "properties": {
          "props": {
            "type": "object",
            "description": "Metadata fields to merge into document props"
          },
          "title": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "New document title"
          },
          "content": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Markdown content to store in the content column. Required when update_strategy is append or merge_llm."
          },
          "type_key": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "New document type key"
          },
          "state_key": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Document state (draft, in_review, ready, published, archived)"
          },
          "description": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Short summary of the document"
          },
          "document_id": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Document UUID (required)"
          },
          "update_strategy": {
            "enum": [
              "replace",
              "append",
              "merge_llm"
            ],
            "type": "string",
            "default": "replace",
            "description": "How to apply content: 'replace' (default), 'append', or 'merge_llm' to intelligently merge with existing content."
          },
          "merge_instructions": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Optional guidance when merging content (e.g., keep headers, preserve tables, integrate research notes). Used with append/merge_llm, but does not replace the required content."
          }
        },
        "additionalProperties": false
      },
      "description": "Update an existing ontology document. Use for edits to titles, states, body markdown, or metadata. For update_strategy \"append\" or \"merge_llm\", include non-empty content/body_markdown/markdown/body/text. merge_instructions alone is not content. Append example: update_onto_document({ document_id, content: \"## Progress Updates\\n\\n- Chapter 2 complete...\", update_strategy: \"append\", merge_instructions: \"Append under Progress Updates; preserve existing sections.\" })"
    },
    {
      "name": "get_document_tree",
      "type": "function",
      "function": {
        "name": "get_document_tree",
        "parameters": {
          "type": "object",
          "required": [
            "project_id"
          ],
          "properties": {
            "project_id": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Project ID to get the document tree for (required)"
            },
            "include_content": {
              "type": "boolean",
              "default": false,
              "description": "Include full document content bodies. Only applies when include_documents is true."
            },
            "include_documents": {
              "type": "boolean",
              "default": false,
              "description": "Include document metadata and unlinked document list. Set true when you need titles or orphaned docs."
            }
          }
        },
        "description": "Get the hierarchical document tree structure for a project.\nReturns the tree structure; set include_documents to include document metadata and unlinked docs.\nDocuments are organized in a wiki-like tree structure with folders (documents that have children) and leaf documents."
      },
      "parameters": {
        "type": "object",
        "required": [
          "project_id"
        ],
        "properties": {
          "project_id": {
            "type": "string",
            "description": "Project ID to get the document tree for (required)"
          },
          "include_content": {
            "type": "boolean",
            "default": false,
            "description": "Include full document content bodies. Only applies when include_documents is true."
          },
          "include_documents": {
            "type": "boolean",
            "default": false,
            "description": "Include document metadata and unlinked document list. Set true when you need titles or orphaned docs."
          }
        }
      },
      "description": "Get the hierarchical document tree structure for a project.\nReturns the tree structure; set include_documents to include document metadata and unlinked docs.\nDocuments are organized in a wiki-like tree structure with folders (documents that have children) and leaf documents."
    },
    {
      "type": "function",
      "function": {
        "name": "move_document_in_tree",
        "parameters": {
          "type": "object",
          "required": [
            "project_id",
            "document_id"
          ],
          "properties": {
            "project_id": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Project UUID (required)"
            },
            "document_id": {
              "type": "string",
              "description": "Document UUID to move (required)"
            },
            "new_position": {
              "type": "number",
              "default": 0,
              "description": "Position among siblings (0-indexed). Omit to place at top."
            },
            "new_parent_id": {
              "type": [
                "string",
                "null"
              ],
              "description": "New parent document ID (null or omitted places the document at root level)."
            }
          }
        },
        "description": "Move or insert an existing document within the project's doc_structure.\nUse this to nest existing or unlinked documents under a parent or reorder siblings.\nIf new_parent_id is null or omitted, the document is placed at root level.\nAlways pass the exact document_id from get_document_tree/list_onto_documents (do not pass document titles)."
      }
    }
  ],

    "entity_id": "04af5167-e41f-4d10-931b-c257c12767bb",
    "project_id": "04af5167-e41f-4d10-931b-c257c12767bb",
    "context_type": "project",
    "project_name": "The Last Ember",
    "lite_sections": [
      {
        "id": "identity_mission",
        "kind": "static",
        "chars": 602,
        "title": "Identity and Mission",
        "source": "lite.static_frame",
        "content": "Who:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.",
        "estimatedTokens": 151
      },
      {
        "id": "operating_strategy",
        "kind": "static",
        "chars": 1019,
        "title": "Operating Strategy",
        "source": "lite.strategy",
        "content": "How to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.",
        "estimatedTokens": 255
      },
      {
        "id": "safety_data_rules",
        "kind": "static",
        "chars": 2477,
        "title": "Safety and Data Rules",
        "source": "lite.safety",
        "content": "- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. If the target is ambiguous, ask before mutating state.\n- Treat permissions, member roles, and access as hard constraints.\n- Preserve document hierarchy rules: documents live in the document tree, not graph edges.\n- Document placement: named research notes, specs, worldbuilding, outlines, or multi-section research go in a dedicated document via create_onto_document, then place it with move_document_in_tree (usually nested under the project context document). Short progress snippets can append to an existing context or progress document.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough — always include the actual content to persist.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.",
        "estimatedTokens": 620
      },
      {
        "id": "capabilities_skills_tools",
        "kind": "static",
        "chars": 3767,
        "title": "Capabilities, Skills, and Tools",
        "source": "lite.static_capability_skill_catalog",
        "content": "Think in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed later in Current Tool Surface.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill metadata:\n- calendar_management: Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context.\n- task_management: Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.",
        "estimatedTokens": 942
      },
      {

  "rendered_dump_text": "========================================\nFASTCHAT V2 PROMPT SNAPSHOT\nStream run: cda746df-5408-41d8-95ff-58bdf2835a86\nSession:    13fc9ea8-13d7-4aee-8e2f-df1cce9bb524\nContext:    project\nEntity ID:  04af5167-e41f-4d10-931b-c257c12767bb\nProject ID: 04af5167-e41f-4d10-931b-c257c12767bb\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\nTools (14): skill_load, tool_search, tool_schema, change_chat_context, get_project_overview, get_onto_project_details, search_project, list_onto_tasks, list_onto_documents, get_onto_document_details, create_onto_document, update_onto_document, get_document_tree, move_document_in_tree\nMessage count: 4\nSystem prompt length: 14936 chars (~3734 tokens)\n========================================\n\n────────────────────────────────────────\nSYSTEM PROMPT\n────────────────────────────────────────\n# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. If the target is ambiguous, ask before mutating state.\n- Treat permissions, member roles, and access as hard constraints.\n- Preserve document hierarchy rules: documents live in the document tree, not graph edges.\n- Document placement: named research notes, specs, worldbuilding, outlines, or multi-section research go in a dedicated document via create_onto_document, then place it with move_document_in_tree (usually nested under the project context document). Short progress snippets can append to an existing context or progress document.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough — always include the actual content to persist.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nThink in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed later in Current Tool Surface.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill metadata:\n- calendar_management: Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context.\n- task_management: Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent project focus:\n- Project: The Last Ember (04af5167-e41f-4d10-931b-c257c12767bb) (planning)\n- Project summary: A young blacksmith MESSAGES\n────────────────────────────────────────\n[1] role=system\n# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. If the target is ambiguous, ask before mutating state.\n- Treat permissions, member roles, and access as hard constraints.\n- Preserve document hierarchy rules: documents live in the document tree, not graph edges.\n- Document placement: named research notes, specs, worldbuilding, outlines, or multi-section research go in a dedicated document via create_onto_document, then place it with move_document_in_tree (usually nested under the project context document). Short progress snippets can append to an existing context or progress document.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough — always include the actual content to persist.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nThink in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed later in Current Tool Surface.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill metadata:\n- calendar_management: Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context.\n- task_management: Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent project focus:\n- Project: The Last Ember (04af5167-e41f-4d10-931b-c257c12767bb) (planning)\n- Project summary: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n- Primary goal: \"Complete first draft of 'The Last Ember'\" (draft)\n- Focus entity: none\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project-scoped assistant that understands the current project and can help move its work forward.\n\n## Location and Loaded Context\n\nLoaded scope:\n- inside The Last Ember (04af5167-e41f-4d10-931b-c257c12767bb)\n- The bounded index below is for orientation and exact IDs only; it is not the full cache.\n- Fetch full entity details before non-obvious writes or when the user asks for complete lists.\n- Product surface and stream turn IDs are captured in dump metadata, not as project facts.\n\nActionable loaded context index       "tool_arguments": {
        "content": "## Progress Update - 2026-04-16\n\n**Chapter 2 Complete**\n- Word count: 4,500\n- Highlight: The scene where Elena discovers the dragon forge went really well.\n\n**Issues to Address in Chapter 2:**\n- Strengthen the dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\n- The pacing in the middle feels slow\n- Add more sensory details about the forge\n\n**Continuity Issue:**\n- Elena's age mentioned as 16 in chapter 1 but 17 in chapter 2. Fix to consistent age (suggest 17).\n\n**Chapter 3 Plans:**\n- Elena's first attempt at magical forging\n- Introduce the Shadow King's herald\n- Foreshadow the prophecy",
        "document_id": "a0487b08-21ef-45b4-9835-a5df42711f2e",
        "update_strategy": "append",
        "merge_instructions": "Append as a new top-level section at the end of the document. Do not alter existing content. Use markdown headers to integrate cleanly."

        "message": "Updated ontology document \"The Last Ember - Project Overview\"",
        "document": {
          "id": "a0487b08-21ef-45b4-9835-a5df42711f2e",
          "props": {
            "body_markdown": "# The Last Ember\n\n## Main Plot\nA young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n\n## Initial Needs\n- Develop main character backstory (orphan, raised by master blacksmith)\n- Create magic system based on metal and fire\n- Map out the kingdom of Aethermoor\n- Write character profiles for the antagonist (The Shadow King)\n- Outline first three chapters\n- Research medieval blacksmithing techniques\n- Design the prophecy that drives the plot\n\n## Progress Update - 2026-04-16\n\n**Chapter 2 Complete**\n- Word count: 4,500\n- Highlight: The scene where Elena discovers the dragon forge went really well.\n\n**Issues to Address in Chapter 2:**\n- Strengthen the dialogue between Elena and Master Thorne\n- The pacing in the middle feels slow\n- Add more sensory details about the forge\n\n**Continuity Issue:**\n- Elena's age mentioned as 16 in chapter 1 but 17 in chapter 2. Fix to consistent age (suggest 17).\n\n**Chapter 3 Plans:**\n- Elena's first attempt at magical forging\n- Introduce the Shadow King's herald\n- Foreshadow the prophecy"
          },

#### Request Message

```text
Research notes for the magic system:

Found interesting parallels with Japanese sword-making traditions - the idea of the smith's spirit entering the blade. Could adapt this: Elena's emotions during forging affect the weapon's properties.

- Anger = fire damage
- Sorrow = ice/frost
- Joy = healing properties
- Fear = defensive shields

Also researching:

- Damascus steel patterns for visual descriptions
- Celtic mythology about smith gods (Goibniu)
- Types of medieval weapons beyond swords

World-building additions:

- The Forge Temples: ancient sites where dragon fire still burns
- Smith's Guild hierarchy and traditions
- The Quenching Ritual: how magical weapons are completed
- Regional differences in forging techniques across Aethermoor
```

  "system_prompt": "# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. If the target is ambiguous, ask before mutating state.\n- Treat permissions, member roles, and access as hard constraints.\n- Preserve document hierarchy rules: documents live in the document tree, not graph edges.\n- Document placement: named research notes, specs, worldbuilding, outlines, or multi-section research go in a dedicated document via create_onto_document, then place it with move_document_in_tree (usually nested under the project context document). Short progress snippets can append to an existing context or progress document.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough — always include the actual content to persist.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nThink in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed later in Current Tool Surface.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill metadata:\n- calendar_management: Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context.\n- task_management: Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent project focus:\n- Project: The Last Ember (04af5167-e41f-4d10-931b-c257c12767bb) (planning)\n- Project summary: A young blacksmith       "content": "# BuildOS Lite Agentic Chat Prompt\n\n\n\nPrompt variant: lite_seed_v1\n\n\n\n## Identity and Mission\n\nWho:\n- You are a proactive project assistant for BuildOS, working for the signed-in user.\n- BuildOS is a graph-based project collaboration system. Projects can contain goals, milestones, plans, tasks, documents, risks, events, members, and relationships.\n\nMission:\n- Help users capture, organize, understand, and advance their project work.\n- Preserve concrete user details, ground answers in available context, and use tools when the answer or action requires current project data.\n- Keep the conversation useful for whatever the user says next; do not overfit the seed prompt to one expected request.\n\n## Operating Strategy\n\nHow to act:\n- Start with the loaded context. If the loaded context is enough, answer without extra tool calls.\n- Treat context zooming as durable state movement. Use change_chat_context early when the latest request should zoom into one resolved project or back out to the workspace.\n- Do not bounce contexts for ambiguous project names, multi-project comparisons, or brief side mentions. Resolve ambiguity first, then shift only when the intended focus is clear.\n- Use direct tools first when they fit. Use discovery tools only when the exact operation or schema is missing.\n- Load a skill when the workflow is multi-step, stateful, or easy to get wrong; do not load full skill playbooks for simple answers.\n- Ask one concise clarification only when the missing detail blocks a safe answer or write.\n- After a tool call, anchor the next step in what the tool actually returned: what changed, where the runtime is now, and what should happen next.\n- Keep scratch reasoning private and make user-facing responses direct.\n\n## Safety and Data Rules\n\n- Do not claim a tool ran unless the runtime supplied a successful tool result.\n- Discovering a tool, loading a schema, reading context, or planning is not completion. Only say an entity was created, updated, moved, merged, archived, deleted, scheduled, or linked after the corresponding write tool succeeded.\n- Pre-tool lead-ins are intent only: say what you will attempt, not that it already happened. Do not state the final outcome, success, or persisted update until all tool calls for that turn have completed.\n- If any write fails and no later retry repairs the same target, state what did not persist and keep the partial-success summary precise. When you cannot execute the requested write at all, say \"I was unable to <requested action>\" and briefly name the blocker so the user knows exactly what did not change.\n- Final responses must match the actual write set: mention every successful write that materially matters, and do not claim task progress, document type, tree placement, or linking when the corresponding tool call did not run or did not succeed.\n- Do not claim a requested document type, tree placement, link, or cross-link unless the successful tool result confirms the actual type or operation.\n- User-visible durable fields (titles, descriptions, document content, project descriptions, props) must contain only final user-visible content. Never let tool-control syntax or argument framing leak into those strings; put control parameters in their own tool arguments, not inside text fields.\n- Do not invent project, task, document, calendar, member, or Libri data that is not in loaded context or tool results.\n- For writes, use exact IDs from context or tool results. If the target is ambiguous, ask before mutating state.\n- Treat permissions, member roles, and access as hard constraints.\n- Preserve document hierarchy rules: documents live in the document tree, not graph edges.\n- Document placement: named research notes, specs, worldbuilding, outlines, or multi-section research go in a dedicated document via create_onto_document, then place it with move_document_in_tree (usually nested under the project context document). Short progress snippets can append to an existing context or progress document.\n- Document append/merge writes require non-empty content. merge_instructions alone is not enough — always include the actual content to persist.\n- When context is incomplete, state the limit and use the narrowest tool that can fill the gap.\n\n## Capabilities, Skills, and Tools\n\nThink in three layers. They work together in sequence:\n\n1. Capability - what BuildOS can do for the user.\n2. Skill - workflow guidance for doing that work well. Skill metadata is preloaded in this prompt; call skill_load when the task is multi-step or easy to get wrong and you need the full markdown playbook.\n3. Tool / Op - the exact execution surface. The current tool names are listed later in Current Tool Surface.\n\nCapabilities:\n- Workspace and project overviews: Get BuildOS-native status snapshots for the whole workspace or one project without assembling generic ontology reads by hand.\n- Project creation: Turn a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- Project graph management: Inspect and update projects, goals, milestones, risks, and relationships across the BuildOS graph.\n- Planning and task structuring: Turn outcomes into plans and tasks, refine existing plans, and connect execution to goals, milestones, and documents.\n- Document workspace management: Create, update, place, and reorganize project documents and task workspace docs without breaking hierarchy rules.\n- Calendar management: Check the calendar, create or reschedule events, cancel events, and manage project calendar mapping.\n- People and profile context: Use user profile context and contact records when personalization or relationship context matters.\n- Project audit: Review project health, structure, blockers, stale work, and missing coverage from project context.\n- Project forecast: Establish likely schedule outcomes, slippage risk, and the strongest drivers of project uncertainty from project context.\n- Web research: Search the web, inspect URLs, and pull in current external information when needed.\n- BuildOS product reference: Explain BuildOS product concepts, usage patterns, and product-specific guidance from internal BuildOS reference tools.\n- Schema and field reference: Inspect field metadata and schema hints when exact model fields or contracts matter.\n\nSkill metadata:\n- calendar_management: Calendar workflow playbook for BuildOS agentic chat. Use for event reads/writes, scope decisions, and project calendar mapping.\n- document_workspace: Project document hierarchy playbook for doc tree operations, unlinked docs, task docs, and document CRUD rules.\n- libri_knowledge: Libri is BuildOS's connected durable library and enrichment system; use this playbook for Libri access questions, stable people or author knowledge, books, categories, authors, and ingested YouTube videos before generic web search.\n- people_context: People context playbook for profile lookup, contact search and updates, candidate resolution, and safe handling of sensitive contact values.\n- plan_management: BuildOS plan workflow playbook for turning a goal or milestone into a durable execution source of truth with scope, timeline, tasks, dependencies, owners, risks, and update rules.\n- project_audit: Project-scoped audit playbook for evaluating structure, documentation, timeline realism, velocity, loose work, duplication, blockers, and strategic gaps while staying in project context.\n- project_creation: Project creation playbook for turning a user idea into the smallest valid BuildOS project payload with inferred name, type_key, props, and only the initial structure the user actually described.\n- project_forecast: Project-scoped forecasting playbook for projecting trajectory, goal attainment, emerging work, schedule risk, assumptions, and next strategic moves while staying in project context.\n- task_management: Task workflow playbook for deciding when work should become a task and how to manage task scope, ownership, schedule, and relationships safely.\n\n## Current Focus and Purpose\n\nCurrent project focus:\n- Project: The Last Ember (04af5167-e41f-4d10-931b-c257c12767bb) (planning)\n- Project summary: A young blacksmith discovers she can forge magical weapons when the kingdom's last dragon dies and darkness threatens the realm.\n- Primary goal: \"Complete first draft of 'The Last Ember'\" (draft)\n- Focus entity: none\n\nUse this seed for:\n- Seed a project-scoped assistant that understands the current project and can help move its work forward.\n\n## Location and Loaded Context\n\nLoaded scope:\n- inside The Last Ember (04af5167-e41f-4d10-931b-c257c12767bb)\n- The bounded index below is for orientation and exact IDs only; it is not the full cache.\n- Fetch full entity details before non-obvious writes or when the user asks for complete lists.\n- Product surface and stream turn IDs are captured in dump metadata, not as project facts.\n\nActionable loaded context index For project zoom-in, pass project_id if known or project_query if the name needs resolution. Ambiguous or missing project matches return candidates and do not change context."
